questions.text: Which of the following cell organelles is responsible for extracting energy from carbohydrates to form ATP?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are cellular structures that read messenger RNA (mRNA) and assemble amino acids into proteins. They can be free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum, facilitating protein synthesis for different cellular needs.

Chloroplasts in plant cells contain pigments like chlorophyll that capture sunlight. This energy is then used in photosynthesis to convert carbon dioxide and water into chemical energy stored in glucose.

Mitochondria are miniature biochemical factories where foodstuffs or respiratory substrates are completely oxidized to carbon dioxide and water. The energy liberated in the process is initially stored in the form of reduced coenzymes and reduced prosthetic groups. The latter soon undergoes oxidation and form energy-rich ATP. ATP comes out of mitochondria and helps perform various energy-requiring processes of the cell, like muscle contraction, nerve impulse conduction, biosynthesis, membrane transport, cell division, movement, etc. Because of the formation of ATP, the mitochondria are called the power houses of the cell.

Lysosomes are organelles that contain digestive enzymes to break down waste materials, cellular debris, and foreign substances. They enable intracellular digestion, recycling components for reuse within the cell.

questions.explanation: The mitochondrion is called the "powerhouse of the cell" because it breaks down carbohydrates through cellular respiration and produces ATP. Ribosomes make proteins, chloroplasts perform photosynthesis, and lysosomes carry out digestion, but ATP formation mainly occurs in mitochondria.

questions.text: Select the mismatch.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gas vacuoles are typically found in certain types of bacteria, aiding in buoyancy. This is not a mismatch.
Large central vacuoles are a feature of plant cells, used for storage and maintaining cell rigidity. This is a mismatch as animal cells have small, scattered vacuoles.
Protists are a diverse group of eukaryotic organisms, including both unicellular and multicellular forms. This is not a mismatch.
Methanogens are a type of archaea, which are prokaryotic organisms capable of producing methane, especially in anaerobic conditions. This is not a mismatch.

questions.explanation: Large central vacuoles are a feature of plant cells, used for storage and maintaining cell rigidity. This is a mismatch as animal cells have small, scattered vacuoles.

questions.text: A cell organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles that contain hydrolytic enzymes capable of breaking down various biomolecules. They function in acidic conditions (pH 4-5) and are involved in processes like intracellular digestion and recycling of cellular components.

Microbodies consist of peroxisomes and glyoxysomes. Peroxisomes contain oxidative enzymes for detoxification processes, while glyoxysomes are involved in lipid metabolism in plants. They do not contain hydrolytic enzymes.
Ribosomes are the cellular structures responsible for protein synthesis. They do not contain hydrolytic enzymes and are not involved in digestion or breakdown processes within the cell.
Mesosomes are infoldings of the plasma membrane found in prokaryotes. They are related to cellular processes such as DNA replication and respiration, and do not contain hydrolytic enzymes.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain powerful hydrolytic enzymes used for intracellular digestion. They break down worn-out organelles, foreign particles, and macromolecules.

questions.text: Which one of the following cell organelles is enclosed by a single membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Lysosomes are single membrane-bound organelles containing hydrolytic enzymes. They break down cellular waste, damaged organelles, and macromolecules, aiding in intracellular digestion and recycling.

The nucleus is enclosed by a double-layered nuclear envelope that separates its contents from the cytoplasm. This membrane regulates the exchange of materials, such as RNA and proteins, between the nucleus and the rest of the cell.

Mitochondria are surrounded by an outer membrane and a highly folded inner membrane called cristae. These folds increase surface area, allowing more space for the electron transport chain and efficient ATP production.
Chloroplasts have a double membrane—an outer and an inner layer—that encloses their internal structures. They carry out photosynthesis, converting light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.explanation: Lysosomes are enclosed by a single membrane and contain hydrolytic enzymes that help in digestion processes within the cell.

questions.text: Which of the following structures is not found in a prokaryotic cell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mesosomes are invaginations of the plasma membrane in prokaryotic cells. They are thought to assist in processes like respiration, DNA replication, and cell division.
All cells are surrounded by a plasma membrane, which separates the cell’s interior from the external environment. It controls the movement of substances in and out, maintaining homeostasis.
Prokaryotic cells do not have a nuclear envelope. Their DNA is concentrated in a nucleoid region, which is not separated from the cytoplasm by a membrane.
Prokaryotic cells have ribosomes that are smaller (70S) than eukaryotic ribosomes. They function in protein synthesis and are freely suspended in the cytoplasm.

questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack a nuclear envelope; their genetic material is located in a region called the nucleoid, not enclosed by a membrane.

questions.text: Cellular organelles with membranes are
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are non-membrane-bound organelles made of ribosomal RNA and proteins. They serve as the site of protein synthesis in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells.

Lysosomes and the Golgi apparatus are enclosed by single membranes, while mitochondria have a double membrane. These membranes help compartmentalize functions like digestion, protein processing, and energy production.

Ribosomes lack a surrounding membrane and float freely in the cytoplasm or on the rough ER. In contrast, nuclei and mitochondria are membrane-bound, which helps compartmentalize their specialized functions.

Ribosomes are non-membrane-bound structures that synthesize proteins. Chromosomes, on the other hand, are composed of DNA and proteins and are located within the nucleus, not as independent organelles.

questions.explanation: These organelles are membrane-bound: lysosomes and the Golgi apparatus have single membranes, while mitochondria have double membranes.

questions.text: DNA is not present in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus serves as the control center of the cell, housing the primary genetic material in the form of linear DNA. It is essential for regulating cellular activities and gene expression.
Mitochondria are known as the powerhouse of the cell and possess their own circular DNA, which is involved in energy production through cellular respiration.
Chloroplasts are organelles found in plant cells that conduct photosynthesis and also contain their own circular DNA, which is crucial for their function.
Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis within the cell, composed of ribosomal RNA and proteins. They do not contain any DNA, making them distinct from the other organelles listed.

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis and are composed of ribosomal RNA and proteins. They do not contain DNA.

questions.text: The structures that are formed by stacking of organised flattened membranous sacs in the chloroplasts are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cisternae are flattened, membrane-bound sacs that make up the Golgi apparatus. They process, modify, and package proteins and lipids, and are not part of chloroplast structure.
Stroma is the fluid-filled interior of a chloroplast that surrounds the thylakoids. It contains enzymes, DNA, and ribosomes needed for the light-independent reactions of photosynthesis.
Cristae are the inner membrane folds of mitochondria that increase surface area for the electron transport chain. They play a key role in ATP production and are not found in chloroplasts.
Grana are stacks of thylakoids within the chloroplasts, where light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis occur. These are the correct structures formed by stacking of flattened membranous sacs.

questions.explanation: In chloroplasts, the grana are the structures formed by stacks of thylakoids, which are flattened membranous sacs. These thylakoid stacks are essential for the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis. On the other hand, Cristae are folds of the inner membrane of mitochondria, involved in cellular respiration, not related to chloroplasts. Stroma is the fluid-filled matrix of the chloroplast where enzymes for photosynthesis are located, not a stacked structure. Cisternae are a series of flattened, membrane-bound sacs that form the Golgi apparatus, not found in chloroplasts.

questions.text: Select the correct matching in the following pairs.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rough ER is primarily involved in protein synthesis due to the presence of ribosomes on its surface, not in glycogen synthesis.

options.text: Rough ER – Oxidation of fatty acids
options.explanation: Rough ER is not involved in the oxidation of fatty acids; it is primarily associated with protein synthesis.
Smooth ER is not responsible for oxidation of phospholipids; it is primarily involved in lipid synthesis and carbohydrate metabolism.
Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) is crucial for the synthesis of lipids, including phospholipids and fatty acids, and is involved in various metabolic processes.

questions.explanation: Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) is crucial for the synthesis of lipids, including phospholipids and fatty acids, and is involved in various metabolic processes.

questions.text: The osmotic expansion of a cell kept in water is chiefly regulated by
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria generate ATP by cellular respiration, providing energy for the cell. They do not regulate osmotic balance, which is mainly managed by the plasma membrane and vacuoles.
Vacuoles are enclosed by a membrane and store water, nutrients, and waste. They help maintain osmotic balance and turgor pressure, supporting the cell’s structure and proper functioning.

Plastids, such as chloroplasts and amyloplasts, are responsible for photosynthesis and storing substances like starch. They do not directly regulate osmotic balance, which is mainly controlled by vacuoles and the plasma membrane.
Ribosomes synthesize proteins by translating mRNA but do not participate in water regulation or maintaining osmotic balance, which is handled by structures like vacuoles and the plasma membrane.

questions.explanation: Vacuoles are membrane-bound organelles that store nutrients and waste products, helping maintain osmotic balance and cell turgor by controlling water uptake.

questions.text: The Golgi complex plays a major role
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids but does not generate energy. Energy production is primarily carried out by mitochondria through cellular respiration.

options.text: In post-translational modification of proteins and glycosylation of lipids
options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus is essential for processing and modifying proteins after synthesis, including adding sugar groups to proteins and lipids. These modifications ensure proteins are functional and correctly targeted within or outside the cell.

options.text: In capturing light energy and converting it into chemical energy
options.explanation: Light energy is captured by chlorophyll in chloroplasts and converted into chemical energy through photosynthesis. The Golgi complex does not participate in energy capture or conversion; its role is in processing and packaging cellular molecules.

options.text: In the intracellular digestion of proteins and carbohydrates
options.explanation: Lysosomes carry out intracellular digestion by breaking down macromolecules, old organelles, and cellular debris using hydrolytic enzymes. The Golgi complex, in contrast, mainly modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids.

questions.explanation: The Golgi complex is integral to the post-translational modification of proteins and the glycosylation of lipids. Proteins synthesized in the rough endoplasmic reticulum and lipids from the smooth endoplasmic reticulum are further processed in the Golgi apparatus. Here, proteins undergo modifications such as folding and cutting, and both proteins and lipids are glycosylated, meaning they are combined with carbohydrates to form glycoproteins and glycolipids, respectively. This is essential for their proper function and transport within the cell.

questions.text: Which one of the following organelles in the figure correctly matches with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Golgi apparatus participates in the formation of glycolipids, but it is not depicted in the figure provided. The question is about identifying the organelle visible in the figure.

The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER), shown in the figure, is involved in protein synthesis due to the presence of ribosomes on its surface. It extends from the nuclear membrane as a network of cisternae.

options.text: Rough endoplasmic reticulum, formation of glycoproteins
options.explanation: The primary role of the rough endoplasmic reticulum is protein synthesis. While it is involved in the initial stages of glycoprotein formation, the final modification occurs in the Golgi apparatus.
The Golgi apparatus is not responsible for protein synthesis. This function is carried out by ribosomes associated with the rough endoplasmic reticulum.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Rough endoplasmic reticulum, protein synthesis. The rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) is clearly depicted in the figure, characterized by its ribosome-studded surface, which is crucial for protein synthesis. The RER plays a significant role in synthesizing proteins intended for secretion or for use within the cell.

questions.text: The term ‘glycocalyx’ is used for:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: A layer present between cell wall and membrane of bacteria
options.explanation: This refers to the periplasmic space in gram-negative bacteria, not the glycocalyx.
The bacterial cell wall is primarily composed of peptidoglycan, which differs from the glycocalyx.

options.text: Bacterial cell engineered to possess N-glycosylated proteins
options.explanation: This pertains to genetic engineering and does not relate to the structure or function of the glycocalyx.

options.text: A layer surrounding the cell wall of bacteria
options.explanation: The glycocalyx is a sticky, gelatinous material that envelops the bacterial cell wall, forming an outer protective layer. This layer can either be a firmly attached 'capsule' or a loosely associated 'slime layer'.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option D: A layer surrounding the cell wall of bacteria. The glycocalyx is a gelatinous, sticky substance that forms an additional outer layer on bacteria, serving as protection and aiding in adherence to surfaces. It can exist as a tightly bound 'capsule' or a more diffuse 'slime layer'.

questions.text: Which of the following types of plastid does not contain stored food material?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chromoplasts are responsible for the vivid colors in plant parts, such as flowers and fruits, due to the presence of carotenoid pigments. They are not involved in food storage but rather in attracting pollinators and aiding in seed dispersal.
Elaioplasts are a type of leucoplast specialized in synthesizing and storing lipids, such as fatty acids and terpenes.
Aleuroplasts are specialized for the storage of proteins, typically found in seeds where they store protein reserves for germination.
Amyloplasts are a type of leucoplast responsible for the synthesis and storage of starch, typically found in roots and tubers.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Chromoplasts. Unlike other plastids, chromoplasts are not involved in the storage of food materials. Instead, they contain pigments that give flowers and fruits their vibrant colors, which is crucial for attracting pollinators and seed dispersers. In contrast, elaioplasts store lipids, aleuroplasts store proteins, and amyloplasts store starch. Each of these plastids plays a role in storing specific types of food materials, unlike chromoplasts.

questions.text: Select the alternative giving correct identification and function of the organelle ‘A’ in the diagram.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Mitochondria - Produces cellular energy in the form of ATP
options.explanation: The labelled organelle is the mitochondria, known as the powerhouse of the cell, producing ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. Its inner membrane and cristae provide the surface for the electron transport chain, enabling efficient energy generation.

options.text: Golgi apparatus - Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids
options.explanation: The mitochondrion is responsible for energy production through ATP synthesis. The Golgi apparatus, in contrast, modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids but does not generate energy.

options.text: Lysosomes - Contains digestive enzymes for breaking down waste
options.explanation: Lysosomes are specialized organelles that break down cellular waste, damaged organelles, and macromolecules using hydrolytic enzymes. They do not generate energy. The labelled organelle, mitochondria, is responsible for producing ATP through oxidative phosphorylation.

options.text: Endoplasmic reticulum - Synthesizes proteins and lipids
options.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is responsible for producing proteins and lipids needed for the cell, including membrane components and secretory proteins. It does not generate ATP. The labelled organelle, mitochondria, is the site of cellular energy production.

questions.explanation: The labelled organelle is the mitochondria, which is the site of cellular respiration and ATP production, making it the 'powerhouse' of the cell. It converts energy stored in food molecules into ATP, which is used to power various cellular processes. The other options describe organelles with different functions: the Golgi apparatus is involved in processing and packaging proteins and lipids, lysosomes contain enzymes for digestion, and the endoplasmic reticulum is involved in protein and lipid synthesis.

questions.text: Select the correct statement from the following regarding the cell membrane.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Na+ and K+ ions move across the cell membrane by passive transport
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because Na+ and K+ ions primarily move across the cell membrane through active transport mechanisms, specifically the sodium-potassium pump, which requires energy to function.

options.text: Proteins make up 60 to 70% of the cell membrane
options.explanation: This statement is misleading; while proteins are essential components of the cell membrane, they typically make up approximately 50 to 60% of the membrane's composition, not 60 to 70%.

options.text: Lipids are arranged in a bilayer with polar heads towards the inner part
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect because in the lipid bilayer, the polar (hydrophilic) heads of the phospholipids face outward towards the aqueous environments both inside and outside the cell, while the hydrophobic tails face inward.

options.text: Fluid mosaic model of cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson
options.explanation: This statement is correct. The fluid mosaic model, proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972, describes the cell membrane as a dynamic structure where a phospholipid bilayer is interspersed with various proteins, allowing for flexibility and movement.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the fluid mosaic model of the cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson. This model illustrates the cell membrane as a flexible and dynamic structure, comprised of a lipid bilayer with proteins embedded within it, which allows for the fluidity necessary for various cellular functions. Option A is incorrect because the transport of Na+ and K+ ions primarily involves active transport, not passive transport. Option B is misleading since proteins constitute about 50 to 60% of the membrane, not 60 to 70%. Option C is also incorrect because the arrangement of lipids in a bilayer has polar heads facing outward, towards the extracellular and cytoplasmic environments, not inward.

questions.text: What is true about ribosomes?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The prokaryotic ribosomes are 80S, where “S” stands for sedimentation coefficient
options.explanation: The prokaryotic ribosomes are actually 70S, not 80S. The 'S' stands for Svedberg unit, which is a measure of sedimentation rate during centrifugation, not a direct measure of size. Eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S.

options.text: These are composed of ribonucleic acid and proteins
options.explanation: Ribosomes are indeed made up of ribonucleic acid (RNA) and proteins, forming two subunits, large and small. They are critical for protein synthesis within the cell.

options.text: These are found only in eukaryotic cells
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Ribosomes are cellular structures that translate mRNA into proteins in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. They differ slightly in size between the two cell types but perform the same essential role of protein synthesis.

options.text: These are self-splicing introns of some RNAs
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Self-splicing introns are non-coding RNA sequences that can remove themselves from RNA molecules. Ribosomes, however, are cellular structures responsible for assembling amino acids into proteins, not involved with splicing RNA.

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are cellular organelles that play a crucial role in protein synthesis. They are composed of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and proteins, forming two subunits: a small subunit and a large subunit. These subunits come together during protein translation to facilitate the assembly of amino acids into polypeptides, based on the sequence of codons in mRNA. Ribosomes are found in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, although their size differs (70S in prokaryotes and 80S in eukaryotes).

questions.text: Which of the following cellular parts is correctly described?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Thylakoids - flattened membranous sacs forming the grana of chloroplasts
options.explanation: Thylakoids are indeed the flattened sac-like membranous structures within chloroplasts. They stack to form grana, where chlorophyll and other pigments reside, facilitating the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis.

options.text: Centrioles - sites for active RNA synthesis
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Centrioles are involved in cell division, particularly in organizing spindle fibers during mitosis and meiosis. RNA synthesis occurs in the nucleolus and the nucleus, not centrioles.

options.text: Ribosomes - those on chloroplasts are larger (80S) while those in the cytoplasm are smaller (70S)
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Chloroplasts contain 70S ribosomes. In contrast, eukaryotic cytoplasm contains 80S ribosomes, while prokaryotic cells have 70S ribosomes.

options.text: Lysosomes - optimally active at a pH of about 8.5
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Lysosomes function optimally at an acidic pH, typically between 4.5 and 5.0, not at a basic pH like 8.5.

questions.explanation: Thylakoids are flattened membranous sacs forming the grana of chloroplasts. These are essential components of chloroplasts, playing a critical role in photosynthesis by housing pigments necessary for capturing light energy.

questions.text: Which one of the following structures is an organelle within an organelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ribosomes are essential for protein synthesis and can be found within mitochondria and chloroplasts, which are organelles that contain their own ribosomes to translate genetic information into proteins. This makes ribosomes unique as they can exist within other organelles.
Peroxisomes are involved in lipid metabolism and detoxification processes but do not exist within other organelles. They are independent organelles with their own roles in the cell.
The endoplasmic reticulum is a major site for protein and lipid synthesis, extending through the cytoplasm, but it does not reside within other organelles.
Mesosomes are not present in eukaryotic cells and are not considered true organelles. They are associated with prokaryotic cells and do not reside within other organelles.

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are critical for protein synthesis and are unique because they can be found within mitochondria and chloroplasts, both of which have their own DNA and protein synthesis machinery, thus qualifying ribosomes as 'organelles within an organelle.' Peroxisomes, endoplasmic reticulum, and mesosomes do not have this characteristic, as they do not reside within other organelles.

questions.text: Peptide synthesis inside a cell takes place in the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chloroplasts capture light energy to produce glucose through photosynthesis in plant cells. They do not synthesize proteins or peptides, as that function is carried out by ribosomes.
Mitochondria produce energy by converting nutrients into ATP through cellular respiration. They are not involved in protein or peptide synthesis, which occurs at ribosomes.

Chromoplasts are plant cell organelles that produce and store pigments, contributing to the coloration of fruits, flowers, and leaves. They do not play a role in protein or peptide synthesis, which is handled by ribosomes.
Ribosomes are the cellular structures where peptide synthesis occurs. They are composed of ribosomal RNA and proteins and are essential for assembling amino acids into proteins.

questions.explanation: Peptide synthesis inside a cell takes place in the ribosomes. Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis, where amino acids are assembled into proteins based on the sequence coded in messenger RNA. They are found in all living cells, both prokaryotic and eukaryotic.

questions.text: An important site for formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Vacuoles are primarily responsible for storing substances such as water, nutrients, and waste products, and maintaining turgor pressure in plant cells. They are not involved in the synthesis or modification of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

The Golgi apparatus is a central organelle in the modification, sorting, and packaging of proteins and lipids. It is crucial for the formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids by adding carbohydrate and lipid moieties to proteins and lipids.
Plastids, such as chloroplasts, are involved in photosynthesis and storage of pigments. They do not participate in the modification or synthesis of glycoproteins and glycolipids.
Lysosomes contain hydrolytic enzymes for breaking down waste materials and cellular debris. They do not play a role in the formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Golgi apparatus. This organelle is key in modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins and lipids for secretion or use within the cell. It is specifically involved in the formation of glycoproteins and glycolipids by attaching carbohydrate and lipid moieties to proteins and lipids. In contrast, vacuoles are for storage, plastids are primarily for photosynthesis and pigment storage, and lysosomes are involved in the breakdown of cellular waste.

questions.text: The figure below shows the structure of a mitochondrion with its four parts labelled A, B, C and D. Select the part correctly matched with its function.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The outer membrane serves as a protective barrier and regulates the movement of ions and molecules in and out of the mitochondrion. However, it does not split to form the inner membrane.

options.text: B (inner membrane) – forms infoldings called cristae
options.explanation: The inner membrane is folded into structures known as cristae, which increase the surface area. This is crucial for ATP production as it houses proteins involved in the electron transport chain.
Crista are folds of the inner membrane that increase its surface area, enabling the housing of more electron transport chain complexes and ATP synthase for ATP production.
The matrix is the innermost compartment of the mitochondrion and contains enzymes for the citric acid cycle, not the respiratory chain which is located in the inner membrane.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is B (inner membrane) – forms infoldings called cristae. The inner membrane of the mitochondrion is folded into cristae, which increase the surface area and allow for efficient ATP production via the electron transport chain and ATP synthase. Option A is incorrect because the outer membrane does not give rise to the inner membrane; instead, it serves as a protective barrier. Option C is incorrect as cristae are not associated with possessing DNA; they are associated with ATP synthesis. Option D is incorrect because while the matrix is the site of the citric acid cycle, the respiratory chain enzymes are actually located in the inner membrane.

questions.text: The plasma membrane consists mainly of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Phospholipids embedded in a protein bilayer
options.explanation: Proteins are not just attached to the surface but are integrated within the phospholipid bilayer of cell membranes. They help with functions like transport, signaling, and structural support, interacting with both the interior and exterior of the cell.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a phospholipid bilayer
options.explanation: The plasma membrane is primarily made up of proteins and lipids, with lipids forming a bilayer that provides a flexible barrier. Proteins are interspersed within this bilayer, performing roles such as transport, signaling, and maintaining the cell’s structure.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a polymer of glucose molecules
options.explanation: Proteins are located within the phospholipid bilayer of cell membranes, not in glucose-based polymers like cellulose or starch. This arrangement allows them to function in transport, signaling, and structural support within the membrane.

options.text: Proteins embedded in a carbohydrate bilayer
options.explanation: Proteins are positioned within the phospholipid bilayer of the cell membrane, not in a carbohydrate bilayer. This structure enables selective permeability and various cellular functions such as transport and communication.

questions.explanation: The plasma membrane consists mainly of lipids (20-40%) and proteins (60-80%). The lipid molecules form a bilayer structure, with proteins embedded within it.

questions.text: The middle lamella is composed mainly of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muramic acid is a unique component of peptidoglycan found in bacterial cell walls. It is absent in plant cells, where the middle lamella is mainly composed of pectin and calcium salts.

The middle lamella, located between adjacent plant cells, is mainly made of calcium pectate. Its pectin-rich composition helps bind cells together, maintaining the structural integrity of plant tissues.
Phosphoglycerides are lipid molecules that make up the structural framework of cell membranes. They are not present in the middle lamella, which is mainly composed of pectin and calcium pectate.
Hemicellulose is a polysaccharide that helps strengthen the primary cell wall by linking cellulose fibers. However, it is not a major constituent of the middle lamella, which mainly contains pectin.

questions.explanation: The middle lamella is primarily composed of calcium pectate, which acts as a cementing agent between plant cells. It is rich in pectin, aiding in cell adhesion.

questions.text: Plasmodesmata are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect adjacent plant cells, allowing transport and communication. They do not aid in movement, as plant cells are generally non-motile.

options.text: Membranes connecting the nucleus with plasmalemma
options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are channels that link the cytoplasm of neighboring plant cells, enabling the exchange of molecules and signals. They do not connect the nucleus to the cell membrane, as their role is strictly intercellular communication.

Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing communication and transport of various substances between them. They are essential for cell-to-cell communication and are involved in the exchange of nutrients, signals, and other molecules between neighboring plant cells.
Plasmodesmata are not lignified or cemented layers; they are dynamic channels that allow direct communication between plant cells.

questions.explanation: Plasmodesmata are microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells, allowing communication and transport of various substances between them. They are essential for cell-to-cell communication and are involved in the exchange of nutrients, signals, and other molecules between neighboring plant cells.

questions.text: The two sub-­units of ribosome remain united at a critical ion level of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The correct option is A) magnesium. Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, a small subunit and a large subunit, which remain united with the help of magnesium ions. Magnesium ions play a crucial role in stabilizing the ribosome's structure and facilitating protein synthesis.

Calcium ions do not play a significant role in keeping the ribosome subunits united. Calcium ions have other important functions in the cell, but they are not directly involved in ribosome assembly or stability.
Copper ions are not involved in the union of ribosome subunits. Copper has essential roles in various enzymes, but it is not a critical ion for ribosome function.
Manganese ions are not critical for keeping the ribosome subunits united. While manganese is an important trace element, it is not directly involved in ribosomal function or structure.

questions.explanation: Ribosomes are composed of two subunits, a small subunit and a large subunit, which remain united with the help of magnesium ions. Magnesium ions play a crucial role in stabilizing the ribosome's structure and facilitating protein synthesis.

questions.text: Select the wrong statement from the following.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain DNA.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both chloroplasts and mitochondria have their own DNA, which allows them to perform certain functions independently of the cell's nuclear DNA.

options.text: The mitochondria are generally smaller than chloroplasts.
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Typically, mitochondria are smaller in size compared to chloroplasts, reflecting their different roles and structures within the cell.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria contain an inner and an outer membrane
options.explanation: This statement is correct. Both organelles have a double-membrane structure, which is fundamental to their functions in energy transformation.

options.text: Both chloroplasts and mitochondria have an internal compartment - the thylakoid space bounded by the thylakoid membrane
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Only chloroplasts have an internal compartment known as the thylakoid space. Mitochondria have a different internal structure, with the inner membrane forming cristae, but no thylakoid space is present.

questions.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Only chloroplasts have an internal compartment known as the thylakoid space. Mitochondria have a different internal structure, with the inner membrane forming cristae, but no thylakoid space is present.

questions.text: Which of the following statements regarding mitochondrial membrane is not correct ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The outer membrane resembles a sieve.
options.explanation: The outer membrane resembles a sieve due to presence of porins.

options.text: The outer membrane is permeable to all kinds of molecules.
options.explanation: The outer membrane of mitochondrion is smooth, freely permeable to all kinds of molecules.

options.text: The enzymes of the electron transfer chain are embedded in the outer membrane.
options.explanation: The enzymes of the electron transfer chain are not embedded in the outer membrane. They are present in inner membrane.

options.text: The inner membrane is highly convoluted forming a series of infoldings.
options.explanation: The inner membrane is usually produced into numerous infolds called cristae (singular crista).

questions.explanation: The outer membrane of mitochondrion is smooth, and freely permeable to all kinds of molecules. It has porin proteins which form channels for the passage of molecules through it. It allows the uptake of substrates and the release of ATP. The inner membrane is semipermeable and regulates the passage of materials into and out of the mitochondrion. It is rich in enzymes and carrier proteins (permeases). It is usually produced into numerous infolds called cristae (singular crista). It bears minute regularly spaced lollipop-­shaped particles known as oxysomes. The rest of the inner membrane contains the electron carrier molecules of the electron transport chain.

questions.text: Ribosomes are produced in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleolus is a region within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized and combined with ribosomal proteins to form the subunits of ribosomes.
The cytoplasm is the gel-like substance within the cell membrane that contains the organelles and is the site for many cellular processes, but it is not where ribosomes are produced.
Mitochondria are the powerhouse of the cell, responsible for energy production through ATP, but they do not produce ribosomes.
The Golgi body modifies and packages proteins and lipids for transport, but it does not play a role in ribosome production.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the nucleolus. The nucleolus is a specialized structure within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized and combined with proteins to form ribosomal subunits. These subunits are then transported out of the nucleus to the cytoplasm, where they function in protein synthesis.

questions.text: Lysosome contains:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxidative enzymes are involved in oxidation reactions and are typically found in organelles like peroxisomes, not lysosomes.
Hydrolytic enzymes are responsible for breaking down various biomolecules and are the primary components of lysosomes, facilitating cellular digestion.
Reductive enzymes are involved in reduction reactions and are not found in lysosomes. They play a role in other cellular processes.
Anabolic enzymes are involved in building up molecules and are not typically found in lysosomes, which are involved in breaking down molecules.

questions.explanation: Hydrolytic enzymes are responsible for breaking down various biomolecules and are the primary components of lysosomes, facilitating cellular digestion.

questions.text: The inner membrane of the mitochondria is, usually, highly convoluted forming a series of infoldings known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thylakoids are membrane-bound compartments inside chloroplasts, not mitochondria. They play a role in photosynthesis.
Lamellae are also related to chloroplasts and function in connecting thylakoids, not found in mitochondria.
Cristae are the infoldings of the inner membrane in mitochondria, increasing surface area for cellular respiration and ATP production.
Grana are stacks of thylakoids found in chloroplasts, involved in the light reactions of photosynthesis, not in mitochondria.

questions.explanation: Cristae are the infoldings of the inner membrane in mitochondria, increasing surface area for cellular respiration and ATP production.

questions.text: Besides giving out secretory vesicles, the Golgi apparatus is also concerned with the formation of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Golgi body is the series of flattened stacks which has two faces: cis and trans. The cis face of a Golgi stack is the end of the organelle where substances enter from the endoplasmic reticulum for processing, while the trans face is where they exit in the form of smaller detached vesicles. The trans face secretes lysosomes and secretory granules.
Formation of plastid, grana of chloroplasts and protein synthesis are not the function of golgi apparatus.
Formation of plastid, grana of chloroplasts and protein synthesis are not the function of golgi apparatus.
Protein synthesis is the function of ribosomes.

questions.explanation: Golgi body is the series of flattened stacks which has two faces: cis and trans. The cis face of a Golgi stack is the end of the organelle where substances enter from the endoplasmic reticulum for processing, while the trans face is where they exit in the form of smaller detached vesicles. The trans face secretes lysosomes and secretory granules.

questions.text: Cell recognition and adhesion occur due to biochemicals of cell membranes named:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Proteins in the cell membrane serve various functions, including acting as receptors and facilitating cell signaling. However, while they play a role in cell adhesion through their interactions with other proteins, they do not specifically handle the recognition tasks attributed to glycoproteins and glycolipids.

Lipids form the structural foundation of cell membranes and ensure membrane fluidity and integrity. They lack the specific recognition sites needed for cell recognition and adhesion, which are primarily functions of glycoproteins and glycolipids.

While proteins and lipids together form the cell membrane, the specific roles in cell recognition and adhesion are carried out by glycoproteins and glycolipids, not just proteins and lipids without carbohydrate attachments.

Glycoproteins and glycolipids have carbohydrate chains that project from the cell surface, allowing them to function as recognition and adhesion molecules. These carbohydrates facilitate specific interactions in cell recognition and adhesion processes.

questions.explanation: Cell recognition and adhesion are primarily mediated by glycoproteins and glycolipids on the cell membrane. These molecules have carbohydrate chains that serve as recognition sites, enabling cells to identify and interact with one another. Glycoproteins and glycolipids are essential in processes such as immune response and tissue formation. In contrast, proteins alone, without carbohydrate modifications, and lipids do not specifically handle the recognition and adhesion tasks. Therefore, the correct answer is glycoproteins and glycolipids.

questions.text: Names of Schleiden and Schwann are associated with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Protoplasm as the physical basis of life
options.explanation: Although the protoplasm concept is important in biology, it is primarily associated with J.H. Huxley and not with Schleiden and Schwann, who are known for cell theory.
Correct. Schleiden and Schwann are credited with the development of cell theory in 1839, which proposes that all living organisms are composed of cells.
This theory is related to the tracing of cell ancestry and development, which is more closely associated with later scientists like Rudolf Virchow, not with Schleiden and Schwann.

options.text: Nucleus functions as control centre of cell
options.explanation: This concept is related to the role of the nucleus in cells, a discovery made by Robert Brown, and not the foundational cell theory by Schleiden and Schwann.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Cell theory. Matthias Schleiden and Theodor Schwann are renowned for formulating the cell theory in 1839, which states that all living organisms are composed of cells. This was a pivotal development in biology. Other options such as the protoplasm theory, theory of cell lineage, and the control function of the nucleus, while important, are attributed to different scientists and concepts outside the core contributions of Schleiden and Schwann.

questions.text: An outer covering membrane is absent over which organelle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleolus is a dense, spherical structure within the nucleus that lacks an outer membrane. It is primarily involved in the synthesis of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and ribosome assembly.
Lysosomes are membrane-bound organelles containing enzymes for digestion. They have a single outer membrane.
Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles responsible for cellular respiration and energy production.
Plastids, such as chloroplasts, have a double membrane and are involved in photosynthesis and storage in plant cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the nucleolus, which lacks an outer membrane. It is a non-membranous structure within the nucleus that plays a critical role in ribosome production. In contrast, lysosomes, mitochondria, and plastids all have membranes: lysosomes have a single membrane, while mitochondria and plastids have double membranes, serving various functions related to energy production, storage, and metabolism.

questions.text: Oxysomes or F0–F1 particles occur on:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because thylakoids are specialized membranes found in chloroplasts, where photosynthesis occurs. Oxysomes are not located here, as they are specific to mitochondria.
This option is incorrect because while mitochondria do house oxysomes, the specific location of oxysomes is on the inner mitochondrial membrane, not just the surface of the mitochondria.

This option is correct. Oxysomes, or F0–F1 particles, are found on the inner mitochondrial membrane. These particles play a crucial role in ATP synthesis as they contain enzyme molecules necessary for this process.
This option is incorrect because chloroplasts are involved in photosynthesis, not ATP synthesis via oxysomes. Oxysomes are specifically associated with the inner mitochondrial membrane.

questions.explanation: Oxysomes, also known as F0–F1 particles, are located on the inner mitochondrial membrane and are essential for ATP synthesis. Each oxysome consists of a base, stalk, and head, and typically measures about 10 nm in length. The number of oxysomes can range from 104 to 105 per mitochondrion, indicating their abundance and importance in cellular energy production. These particles feature a phospholipid core and a protein cortex and contain ATP synthase enzymes, which are critical for converting ADP and inorganic phosphate into ATP. The incorrect options relate to structures that either do not contain oxysomes or are functionally different, such as thylakoids in chloroplasts, which are involved in photosynthesis, and the general mitochondrial surface, which does not specify the inner membrane where oxysomes are specifically located.

questions.text: Cell wall shows:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cell wall is completely permeable, allowing most substances to pass freely in and out of the cell. Its primary function is structural support, not regulation of substance passage.
This option is incorrect. Semipermeability refers to selective passage of substances, a property of the cell membrane, not the cell wall.
This option is incorrect. Differential permeability suggests selective transport, which is characteristic of the cell membrane, not the cell wall.
This option is incorrect. An impermeable barrier would not allow passage of any substances, which is not true for the cell wall.

questions.explanation: The cell wall is completely permeable, allowing most substances to pass freely in and out of the cell. Its primary function is structural support, not regulation of substance passage.

questions.text: Ribosomes were discovered by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Camillo Golgi is known for discovering the Golgi apparatus, an organelle involved in modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins. He did not discover ribosomes.
Keith Porter is credited with pioneering the use of electron microscopy in cell biology, but he did not discover ribosomes.
Edmund De Robertis is known for his work in developmental biology, particularly in relation to vertebrate development, but he was not involved in the discovery of ribosomes.
George Palade discovered ribosomes in animal cells and his significant contributions to cell biology earned him a Nobel Prize. Ribosomes are also known as Palade particles.

questions.explanation: George Palade discovered ribosomes in animal cells in 1955, leading to them being referred to as Palade particles. The other options, Golgi, Porter, and De Robertis, although influential in their respective fields, were not responsible for the discovery of ribosomes. Golgi is known for the Golgi apparatus, Porter for electron microscopy, and De Robertis for developmental biology.

questions.text: Fluid mosaic model of cell membrane was put forward by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Danielli-Davson model is an earlier cell membrane model that suggested a protein-lipid sandwich, which is less accurate compared to the fluid mosaic model.
Singer and Nicolson proposed the fluid mosaic model in 1972, which is currently the most accepted model for cell membrane structure, describing proteins embedded in a fluid lipid bilayer.
Garner and Allard are not associated with any major cell membrane model. This option is incorrect in the context of the fluid mosaic model.
Watson and Crick are famous for discovering the structure of DNA, not for any cell membrane model.

questions.explanation: The fluid-mosaic model of the cell membrane was proposed by Singer and Nicolson in 1972. This model describes the cell membrane as a dynamic and fluid structure with proteins embedded in or associated with a lipid bilayer. The proteins can move laterally within the lipid layer, contributing to the 'mosaic' nature. This model accounts for the semi-permeability and functionality of the membrane. Other options, such as Danielli and Davson, presented earlier models, which have been largely replaced by the fluid-mosaic model. Garner and Allard are not relevant to this context, and Watson and Crick are known for their work on DNA structure, not cell membranes.

questions.text: Polyribosomes are aggregates of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While ribosomes are composed of rRNA and proteins, polyribosomes specifically refer to multiple ribosomes attached to a single mRNA strand, not just ribosomes and rRNA.
This option is incorrect. Polyribosomes are not made up solely of rRNA. They consist of several ribosomes translating a single mRNA strand, which involves both mRNA and ribosomal components.
This option is incorrect. Peroxisomes are distinct organelles involved in metabolic processes, not related to the aggregation of ribosomes or protein synthesis directly.

options.text: Several ribosomes held together by a string of mRNA
options.explanation: This option is correct. Polyribosomes, or polysomes, are complexes where multiple ribosomes are attached to a single mRNA molecule, facilitating efficient protein synthesis by simultaneously translating the same mRNA strand.

questions.explanation: Polyribosomes, also known as polysomes, are clusters of multiple ribosomes attached to a single mRNA molecule. This structure allows several ribosomes to simultaneously translate the mRNA sequence into polypeptide chains, thus enhancing the efficiency of protein synthesis. Option A and B are incorrect because they do not involve mRNA, which is essential for the structure and function of polyribosomes. Option C is incorrect as peroxisomes are separate organelles with different functions related to metabolism and do not participate in forming polyribosomes.

questions.text: Plasma membrane is made of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not completely correct. The plasma membrane, also known as the cell membrane, is indeed composed of proteins and carbohydrates in addition to lipids. The carbohydrates are often present as part of glycoproteins or glycolipids on the outer surface of the membrane and play roles in cell recognition and signaling.

This option is partially correct. The plasma membrane is primarily composed of proteins and lipids. The lipids in the membrane form a phospholipid bilayer, which provides the basic structure of the membrane. Proteins are embedded in the lipid bilayer and have various functions, such as transport, cell signaling, and structural support. However, traces of carbohydrates are also present in addition to proteins and lipids.

This option is correct. The plasma membrane is a dynamic and selectively permeable barrier that surrounds the cell, and it is made up of three main types of biomolecules: proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates. The lipids, mainly phospholipids, form a bilayer, while the proteins are embedded in the lipid bilayer or extend through it. The carbohydrates are often found on the outer surface of the membrane as part of glycoproteins or glycolipids.

options.text: Proteins, some nucleic acids and lipids
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While proteins and lipids are indeed key components of the plasma membrane, nucleic acids, such as DNA and RNA, are not part of the membrane's structure. Nucleic acids are primarily located in the cell nucleus or other specific cellular compartments, and they are not a fundamental part of the plasma membrane.

questions.explanation: The plasma membrane is a dynamic and selectively permeable barrier that surrounds the cell, and it is made up of three main types of biomolecules: proteins, lipids, and carbohydrates. The lipids, mainly phospholipids, form a bilayer, while the proteins are embedded in the lipid bilayer or extend through it. The carbohydrates are often found on the outer surface of the membrane as part of glycoproteins or glycolipids.

questions.text: What is an example of a semi-autonomous organelle found in both animal and plant cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a complex network of membranes involved in the synthesis, folding, and transport of proteins and lipids. It is present in both animal and plant cells, but it is not considered a semi-autonomous organelle. So, this option is incorrect.

Chloroplasts are semi-autonomous organelles found only in plant cells and some protists. They are responsible for photosynthesis, the process by which plants convert sunlight into energy-rich molecules (glucose) using chlorophyll. Chloroplasts contain their own DNA and ribosomes, allowing them to synthesize some of their proteins independently, which makes them semi-autonomous. So, this option is also incorrect.

The Golgi apparatus is an organelle involved in processing, modifying, and sorting proteins and lipids for transport to various destinations within or outside the cell. It is found in both animal and plant cells, but it is not considered a semi-autonomous organelle. So, this option is also incorrect.

This option is correct. Mitochondria are semi-autonomous organelles found in both animal and plant cells. They are often referred to as the "powerhouses" of the cell because they are responsible for generating most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which is used as a source of chemical energy. Mitochondria have their own circular DNA and ribosomes, allowing them to produce some of their proteins independently. This characteristic of having their own genetic material and the ability to self-replicate makes mitochondria semi-autonomous organelles.

questions.explanation: Mitochondria are semi-autonomous organelles found in both animal and plant cells. They are often referred to as the "powerhouses" of the cell because they are responsible for generating most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate (ATP), which is used as a source of chemical energy. Mitochondria have their own circular DNA and ribosomes, allowing them to produce some of their proteins independently. This characteristic of having their own genetic material and the ability to self-replicate makes mitochondria semi-autonomous organelles.

questions.text: All of the following are common molecules in membranes of all type of cells except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Globular proteins, also known as globular or soluble proteins, are three-dimensional, compactly folded proteins that are soluble in water. These proteins perform a wide range of functions within cells, such as enzymatic activities, signal transduction, transport of molecules, and structural support. Globular proteins are present in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell membranes. Hence, this option is incorrect.

Cholesterol is a type of lipid that is a major component of eukaryotic cell membranes. It helps maintain membrane fluidity and stability by interacting with phospholipids. Cholesterol is present in large amounts (around 20-40 mol%) in eukaryotic plasma membranes, but it is universally absent in the membranes of prokaryotes. Therefore, this option is correct as cholesterol is present in eukaryotic cell membranes but not in the prokaryotic cell membranes.

Carbohydrates are commonly found in cell membranes of both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells in the form of glycoproteins and glycolipids. These carbohydrates play important roles in cell-cell recognition, cell adhesion, and other cellular functions. Hence, this option is also incorrect.

This option is incorrect because phospholipids are a fundamental component of all cell membranes, both prokaryotic and eukaryotic. They are amphipathic molecules, meaning they have both hydrophilic (polar) and hydrophobic (nonpolar) regions. Phospholipids spontaneously arrange themselves in a bilayer structure with their hydrophobic tails facing inward and hydrophilic heads facing outward, creating the basic framework of the cell membrane.

questions.explanation: Cholesterol is a type of lipid that is a major component of eukaryotic cell membranes. It helps maintain membrane fluidity and stability by interacting with phospholipids. Cholesterol is present in large amounts (around 20-40 mol%) in eukaryotic plasma membranes, but it is universally absent in the membranes of prokaryotes. Therefore, the correct option is cholesterol as it is not present in all types of cell membranes.

questions.text: Which of the following is not a characteristic feature of the animal cell membrane?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The animal cell membrane is crucial for maintaining cellular homeostasis by regulating the exchange of substances, thereby balancing the cell's internal environment.
This option is incorrect. The cell membrane's selective permeability allows it to regulate the passage of molecules, ensuring that necessary substances enter and harmful substances are kept out.

This option is correct. Unlike plant cells, animal cells lack a cell wall, making them prone to osmotic lysis. In a hypotonic environment, water can enter the cell, potentially causing it to swell and burst, as the membrane alone cannot prevent this.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that the animal cell membrane does not prevent osmotic lysis. Animal cells lack a cell wall, which in plants provides additional protection against osmotic pressure. In hypotonic environments, water can freely enter animal cells, leading to swelling and potential bursting. The other options describe actual functions of the cell membrane: providing some mechanical support, maintaining homeostasis, and regulating molecule passage.

questions.text: Transport of glucose across the cell membrane occurs by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucose is a large polar molecule and large polar molecules require special transporters or facilitators [gluts in case of glucose], therefore they cannot move across the cell membrane through simple diffusion.

Glucose is a large polar molecule and large polar molecules require special transporters or facilitators [gluts in case of glucose], therefore they move across the cell membrane through facilitated diffusion

Osmosis is the passive movement of molecules in presence of water, since glucose is a large molecule it cannot cross the membrane simply by water movement. moreover osmosis is mainly used for the movement of water hence making the option incorrect.

Primary active transport is a process that moves molecules against their concentration gradient across a cell membrane by using metabolic energy, primarily from ATP hydrolysis. Glucose transport is a quick process therefore not requiring any expenditure of energy making the option incorrect.

Secondary active transport moves molecules against their concentration gradient by using the energy stored in another molecule's electrochemical gradient, which was established by primary active transport. Glucose transport is a quick process therefore not requiring any expenditure of energy making the option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The transport of glucose requires special carrier proteins to take place since glucose is a very large polar molecule that cannot diffuse across the cell membrane without a facilitator. Therefore, facilitated diffusion of glucose occurs through GLUT transporters present in most body cells, so the answer is B.Glucose may also be transported via secondary active transport in certain scenarios such as sodium glucose cotransporters present in the intestinal epithelium or proximal convoluted tubule cells as glucose transport is driven by the concentration gradient of sodium created by the action of the Na/K ATPase pump, but the question did not specify so facilitated diffusion is preferred.

questions.text: Identify the incorrect statement from the following:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Apical growth increases the length of stems and roots
options.explanation: Option A is not incorrect because the apical meristems give rise to primary growth (lengthening) and occur at the tips of the roots and shoots. Growth at these regions is due to a combination of cell enlargement and repeated cell division (mitosis and cytokinesis) Differentiation of the dividing meristem gives rise to a variety of stem tissues and structures – including leaves and flowers.

options.text: Xylem is situated on the outer side of the cambium ring and the phloem on the inner side
options.explanation: The transverse section of the dicot stem shows that the vascular tissue is arranged in a ring around the central region (pith). Each individual vascular bundle consists of xylem situated on the inner corner, followed by cambium. Finally, the phloem tissue is present at the outer tip. Therefore, option B is the answer since the statement is factually erroneous.

options.text: Secondary growth increases the diameter of stems and roots
options.explanation: Option C is correct because secondary growth is the growth that results from cell division in the cambia or lateral meristems and that causes the stems and roots to thicken.

options.text: The cells in the elongation phase don't divide
options.explanation: Option D correct because the cells present next to meristematic cells are present in the elongation phase. These cells elongate due to enlargement of the vacuole present in the cell. The cell enlargement and new cell wall formation take place in these cells present in the zone of elongation, but the cells do not divide.

options.text: The cells informative regions are closely packed together
options.explanation: Option E is correct because the organelles inside a cell are specialised to perform their tasks. The nucleus consists of closely packed DNA in chromatin bodies as well as nucleolus and controls the information of the cell.

questions.explanation: In a vascular cambium, the xylem is placed on the inner side while the phloem is placed on the outer side, option B states the opposite.

questions.text: In roots, the apoplast pathway of water is disrupted when water reaches:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Plasmodesmata are the microscopic channels that connect the cytoplasm of adjacent plant cells. They do not have any kind of disruption, moreover plasmodesmata is mainly involved in symplast pathway
Endodermis contain Casparian strips that are made up of suberin and lignin. Suberin has waxy structure therefore disrupting the flow of water.

The cortex in plant anatomy refers to the outermost layer of a stem or root, composed of various cell types. It provides mechanical support, helps regulate water movement, and may contain chloroplasts for photosynthesis in certain plant parts.

In plant anatomy, the pith is the central, often soft, spongy tissue found in the center of stems and certain plant structures. It primarily consists of parenchyma cells and plays a role in storage and support.

questions.explanation: The cell-to-cell pathway is a continuous flow domain interrupted at the endodermis, which has casparian strips that is waxy and sticky in nature therefore disrupting the flow of water.

questions.text: Which diagram illustrates the process of active transport?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A shows passive transport, where molecules move from high to low concentration without energy use.
Option B depicts facilitated diffusion, which also moves molecules down their concentration gradient with the help of a protein carrier, but without energy.
Option C illustrates osmosis, the movement of water molecules through a selectively permeable membrane from low to high solute concentration, also not requiring energy.

Active transport is the movement of molecules across a cell membrane from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration—against the concentration gradient. Active transport requires cellular energy to achieve this movement. In Option D, the molecules enter the Root Hair Cell although the mineral ions are in higher concentration inside the cell.

questions.explanation: Option D is correct because it illustrates active transport, where molecules move against their concentration gradient, requiring energy input, typically from ATP. The diagram likely shows molecules moving into a cell despite a higher concentration inside, indicative of active transport. Option A is incorrect as it shows passive movement, Option B is incorrect as it shows facilitated diffusion, and Option C shows osmosis, all of which do not require energy for the movement of molecules.

questions.text: The diagram shows a tree trunk, with a ring of bark, which includes the phloem removed.The tree will eventually die because this action cuts off the supply of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mineral salts are transported via the xylem, not the phloem.
Organic nutrients, such as sugars, are transported from the leaves to other parts of the plant via the phloem.
Oxygen is not transported through phloem; it is taken up by the roots from the soil.
Water is transported through the xylem, not the phloem.

questions.explanation: Organic nutrients such as sugar molecules are transported through phloem tubes,cutting off this tube cuts off this organic nutrients supply. Moreover, Oxygen supply to the roots is through oxygen dissolved in soil.Mineral salts and water supply to the leaves is through xylem vessels.

questions.text: In an isotonic solution there would be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In an isotonic solution, the concentration of solutes inside the cell is equal to the concentration of solutes outside the cell. This equality means water moves in and out of the cell at equal rates, resulting in no net movement of water.

options.text: Net movement of water into the cell
options.explanation: This occurs when the external solution is hypotonic, meaning it has a lower concentration of solutes compared to the inside of the cell, causing water to move into the cell.

options.text: Net movement of water out of the cell
options.explanation: This occurs in a hypertonic solution, where the external environment has a higher concentration of solutes than the inside of the cell, causing water to move out of the cell.
Cell bursting, or lysis, typically occurs when a cell is placed in a hypotonic solution, leading to excessive water intake. In an isotonic solution, this does not happen.

questions.explanation: In an isotonic solution there is a movement of water into and out of the cell but no net movement of water into or out of the cell as they contain the same concentration of water and solutes as the cell cytoplasm.

questions.text: Translocation of organic solutes in plants takes place through:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The function of the companion cell is to load sugar and amino acids into sieve elements. These cells use transmembrane proteins to take up these molecules by active transport. Their main function is to provide energy to the sieve cells

Fibers are elongated cells with tapering ends and very thick, heavily lignified cell walls. Fiber cells are dead at maturity and function as support tissue in plant stems and roots. They are present in xylem and play part in water transportation.
Sieve tubes are a component of the phloem in plants. They function as the vessel that transports (translocation) organic food and other material across the plant

Vessels form an efficient system for transporting water (including necessary minerals) from the root to the leaves and other parts of the plant. They are part of xylem and are involved in water transportation.

questions.explanation: Sieve tubes are elongated living cells of Phloem which are responsible for the transport of organic solutes. They work in coordination with companion cells for the transportation of food.

questions.text: Movement of ions and large molecules with the help of protein molecules in and out of the cell is called _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of substances down the concentration gradient ( generally small molecules) without the expenditure of energy.

Facilitated diffusion is the passive movement of molecules down the concentration gradient with the help of transport proteins (as these molecules/ions are incapable of moving through cell membranes directly). It is used for large molecules and ions.
Passive transport is a very generalized term used for all of diffusion, osmosis and facilitated diffusion.
Passive movement of water molecules down the water potential gradient across a semi-permeable membrane.

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of molecules down the concentration gradient whereas osmosis is the passive movement of water down the water potential gradient across a semi-permeable membrane. On the contrary, facilitated diffusion is the passive movement of molecules down the concentration gradient with the help of transport proteins (as these molecules/ions are incapable of moving through cell membranes directly).

questions.text: A destarched leaf on a potted plant was covered with Black (A) Transparent (B) strips of paper as shown in figure. After 6 hours of exposure to sunlight the leaf was removed from the plant and tested for starch. Which are the following will be the correct observation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The portion covered with black strips of paper had no sunlight and hence no photosynthesis, it would not turn blue black after the test. Because sunlight is necessary for photosynthesis and chromoplast which is needed to give colours .

options.text: Only B portion turned blue black
options.explanation: B portion and the area uncovered by any strips turns blue black as it performs photosynthesis

options.text: Only A & B portions remained colourless and the rest of the leaf turned blue black
options.explanation: A portion remains colourless but B is blue black along with the rest of the leaf

options.text: Only A remained colourless and the rest of the leaf turned blue black
options.explanation: A remained colourless due to the blockage of sunlightthe leaf was covered with black (A) strips of paper, it would have blocked sunlight from reaching the leaf. Without sunlight, photosynthesis cannot occur, and the leaf would not produce starch.

questions.explanation: A remained colourless due to the blockage of sunlight the leaf was covered with black (A) strips of paper, it would have blocked sunlight from reaching the leaf. Without sunlight, photosynthesis cannot occur, and the leaf would not produce starch.

questions.text: The main cause of creation of tension between water molecules is _ which helps to make water chains pulled up xylem sap.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Root pressure is a minor force generated in plant roots that aids in the upward transport of water and nutrients. However, it is not the primary force responsible for creating tension between water molecules in xylem.

Imbibition refers to the absorption of water by porous materials, like seeds or plant tissues, due to capillary action. It does not create tension between water molecules for upward movement in the xylem.

Water potential is a measure of the potential energy of water in a system, influencing water movement. While it is crucial for understanding water flow, it does not directly explain the tension between water molecules in xylem.

Hydrogen bonding occurs when water molecules are attracted to each other, creating cohesion and enabling the formation of continuous water columns. This is essential for pulling water up through the xylem against gravity.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Hydrogen bonding. Hydrogen bonds form between water molecules, creating a cohesive force that helps maintain a continuous column of water in the plant's xylem. This tension is crucial for the ascent of sap, as it allows water to be pulled up through the plant against gravity.Root pressure is a minor force and does not account for the tension required for water movement over long distances in tall plants. Imbibition involves water absorption by porous materials and is unrelated to the tension in water columns. Water potential governs water movement direction but does not explain the cohesive tension between water molecules.

questions.text: Identify the only cells in leaf epidermis, which are NOT connected to other cells by plasmodesmata and are involved in photosynthesis.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Epidermal cells are connected by plasmodesmata. Epidermal cells are the primary protective outer layer of plant structures, forming a single layer of tightly packed cells that cover the stem, root, leaf, flower, and seed.

Spongy mesophyll cells allows interchange of gases and not part of the epidermis. Spongy mesophyll cells are a type of plant cells found within the mesophyll layer of a leaf. The mesophyll layer is responsible for photosynthesis and are connected by plasmodesmata.

Guard cells are not connected by plasmodesmata and take part in photosynthesis. Guard cells are specialized cells found in the epidermis of plant leaves and stems. They are named so because of their role in regulating the opening and closing of small pores called stomata.
Palisade mesophyll layer is not part of the epidermis. Palisade mesophyll cells are a specialized type of plant cells found in the mesophyll layer of plant leaves.

questions.explanation: Guard cells, containing chloroplast, are a part of the leaf’s epidermal layer ( the epidermis). They, indeed, do not have plasmodesmata (small channels that directly connect the cytoplasm of neighboring plant cells to each other). Spongy and Palisade mesophyll are not part of the epidermal layer and contain Plasmodesmata. questions.text: Which of the following is the most widely accepted theory for the ascent of sap?

questions.explanation: There are many theories explaining the ascent of sap, including:1. Cohesion-Tension Theory: The theory states that transpiration pull or tension, the cohesion property of water, and hydration of the cell walls (i.e., adhesion) are collectively responsible for the ascent of sap.2. Root pressure theory: The theory states that the phenomenon of root pressure is responsible for the ascent of sap.3. Imbibition: According to this theory, the water rises by imbibition through the thick walls of the xylem cells as well as those of the sclerenchyma of the phloem. The forces of imbibition range from 100 to 1000 atm, and this would seem adequate for carrying water to any required distance.Out of all these, the cohesion-tension theory is the most widely accepted.
